content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 11, 1993


Anne Stausboll
Staff Counsel
Legal Office
Public Employees' Retirement System
P.O. Box 942707
Sacramento, California  94229-2707

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-93-080

Dear Ms. Stausboll:

     This is in reply to your request that we review for accuracy
a draft memorandum from the General Counsel of the Public
Employees' Retirement System ("PERS") to members of the PERS board
concerning the Political Reform Act's (the "Act")¹ requirements
concerning honoraria and gifts. Since your request is of a
general nature, we treat it as one for informal advice.²

     The memorandum is generally accurate and covers the main
rules governing the acceptance of honoraria and gifts. I have
itemized my comments below:

     (1) Page 1 of the memorandum, second
paragraph, line 9 - The $250 gift limit has
now been adjusted by the Commission to $270.
The amount, which is based on the Consumer
Price Index, will be revised again, effective
January 1, 1995.

     (2) Attachment A, page A-2, first paragraph,
line 2 - Same comment as above regarding the
$250 gift limit. You also will probably want

_______________
1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2    Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95812 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-93-080
Page 2

to change the footnote referenced in this
paragraph.

(3) Attachment A, page A-2, second paragraph,
item (2), first paragraph - In addition to
return of a gift to the donor or donation to
charity (without tax deduction) within 30
days, a gift may also be "negated" by donation
to a government agency within 30 days. (See
Commission Regulation 18726.1(b)(3).3 )

(4) Attachment A, page A-2, second paragraph,
item (2), second paragraph - Though it is
accurate in some circumstances to state that
the return of gifts exception set forth in the
first paragraph under item (2) does not apply
to passes or tickets, this second paragraph
under item (2), without further explanation,
gives the impression that passes and tickets
cannot be returned and "negated" as gifts.

The return exception does apply to passes
or tickets if they are not used and are
returned to the donor (or given to charity or
the government without taking a tax deduction)
within 30 days of receipt. In addition, under
Regulation 18946.1, an unused single-admission
ticket or pass and the unused portion of a
repeated-admission ticket or pass is not a
gift for purposes of reporting or gift limits.
However, if the ticket or pass has a future
use, then the value of that potential use must
be considered to determine if the official has
a conflict of interest. Also the gift is
still attributed to the official if
the official gives the ticket or pass to
another person or permits that person to
partially use the ticket or pass.

(5) Attachment A, pages A-4 and A-5,
discussion of "Travel and Lodging" - The
Commission has decided to retain its rules,
which predated the new ethics legislation,
concerning whether travel, accommodations and
meals provided in connection with a speech, or
in connection with participation in a panel or
seminar, are gifts.

_______________
3 This regulation will eventually be amended and renumbered
consistent with the Commission's most recently adopted gift
regulations.

Our File No. I-93-080
Page 3


Under Regulation 18950.3, free admission,
refreshments, non-cash nominal benefits,
necessary transportation, lodging and meals
provided to an official when the official
makes a speech or participates in a panel or
seminar in California are not gifts. For
speeches and participation in panels and
seminars outside California, only the cost of
transportation is a gift. In neither event
will any of these items be considered
honoraria.

On this basis, you may either want to
modify the last paragraph on page A-5 or
redraft the materials entirely. Admittedly,
the area of travel is confusing. We have
found that the best approach to explaining it
is to first ask whether the travel,
accommodations and meals are gifts at all and,
if yes, then ask whether the $270 limit
applies.

(6) Attachment A, page A-5, second full
paragraph, line 2 - The reference to the $250
gift limit should be changed to $270.

I hope that these comments have been of assistance. If you
have further questions, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel,

[signature]
By: Scott Hallabrin
Senior Counsel


JM:SH:dl

sections.question_synthetic: Is a draft PERS memorandum to board members regarding Political Reform Act requirements for honoraria and gifts accurate and compliant with applicable regulations?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The memorandum is generally accurate and covers main rules governing honoraria and gifts acceptance, though the FPPC provides six specific comments regarding outdated gift limits ($250 should be $270), clarifications on gift negation procedures, passes/tickets treatment, and travel/lodging provisions under Regulation 18950.3.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Informal advice letter format (no formal Q/A sections); document contains detailed line-by-line comments on a draft memorandum rather than responding to a traditional legal question; immunity disclaimer included for informal advice per Government Code Section 83114.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is a draft PERS memorandum to board members regarding Political Reform Act requirements for honoraria and gifts accurate and compliant with applicable regulations?

CONCLUSION: The memorandum is generally accurate and covers main rules governing honoraria and gifts acceptance, though the FPPC provides six specific comments regarding outdated gift limits ($250 should be $270), clarifications on gift negation procedures, passes/tickets treatment, and travel/lodging provisions under Regulation 18950.3.

embedding.summary: FPPC informal advice letter responding to PERS's request to review a draft memorandum on Political Reform Act requirements for honoraria and gifts. The FPPC finds the memorandum generally accurate but provides six itemized corrections and clarifications, primarily regarding updated gift limits, gift negation procedures, and travel expense treatment.
